votes.reason: The Python code accurately translates the Coq logic, including the fuel-based digit extraction, string conversions, and list slicing for the circular shift.
The Python code faithfully translates the Coq logic, including the fuel-based digit extraction and the string manipulation for the circular shift.
The Python code accurately translates the Coq implementation, including the fuel-based digit extraction, list manipulations, and string conversions.

The Python code faithfully implements the same logic as the Coq code. The to_digits function converts a number to its digit list, circular_shift_impl performs the same conditional logic (reverse if shift > length, otherwise rotate right by shift mod length), and the spec simply checks equality with the implementation result. Both handle the x=0 case identically and use the same comparison (length < shift vs len <? shift in Coq).

The Python code faithfully implements the same logic as the Coq code. Both to_digits functions convert a natural number to a list of digits (with special case for 0), both circular_shift_impl functions apply the same logic: if x=0 return "0", if length < shift return reversed digits, otherwise compute effective_shift and rotate accordingly. The comparison operators (<? vs <, =? vs ==) and list operations (skipn/firstn vs slicing, rev vs reversed) produce equivalent results on concrete inputs.

The Python code faithfully implements the same logic as the Coq code. The to_digits function converts a natural number to a list of digits, circular_shift_impl performs the same conditional logic (reverse if shift > length, otherwise rotate right by effective_shift), and the spec simply checks equality with the implementation result. The iterative Python version of to_digits_fuel produces the same digit list as the recursive Coq version.

Python mirrors the Coq logic on naturals: digits extraction matches (including fuel behavior with fuel=n), string conversion aligns, and circular_shift_impl handles x=0, shift<=len (rotation via modulo), and shift>len (reverse) exactly the same.
Python mirrors Coq for naturals: same digit extraction order, string mapping, and rotation logic (reverse when shift>len, else rotate by shift%len; x=0 handled).

Python mirrors Coq’s digit extraction, string conversion, and shift logic (including reverse when shift > length, modulo-based rotation otherwise, and handling x=0). For nonnegative integers, results match on all inputs.
